Here the focus is in particular on operations of groups on vector spaces. Nevertheless, groups acting on other groups or on sets are also considered. More details appear in the section on permutation representations. Thus, without loss of generality, we can study vector spaces over C . {\displaystyle \mathbb {C} .}

Theorem. (Maschke) Let ρ : G → GL ( V ) {\displaystyle \rho :G\to {\text{GL}}(V)} be a linear representation where V {\displaystyle V} belongs to a finite-dimensional representation space.

Let ρ : G → GL 2 ( C ) {\displaystyle \rho :G\to {\text{GL}}_{2}(\mathbb {C} )} be the group homomorphism defined by ρ {\displaystyle \rho ({0},{0})={\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix}}.}

A map between two representations ρ {\displaystyle (\rho ,V_{\rho }),\,(\tau ,V_{\tau })} of the same group is often visualized by the following diagram.

Such a map is also called equivariant. The kernel, image and cokernel of T {\displaystyle T} are again representations.

Let χ {\displaystyle \chi } be the character of the representation V, {\displaystyle V,} then we have χ {\displaystyle (\chi |\chi )\in \mathbb {N} _{0}.}
